text: The surgeon divides the posterior fundal attachments to fully mobilize the fundus, separating attachments between the angle of His and the left crus. The plane between the crus and the esophagus is identified, and the gastrohepatic ligament is divided as the dissection progresses towards the right crus.

The surgeon identifies and develops the plane between the esophagus and the right cruse, carefully dissecting to avoid injury to the right vagus nerve, which is visible coursing from the right lateral side and deviating posteriorly along the esophagus. The phrenoesophageal membrane is divided, and further dissection clearly defines the anatomical structures of the stomach, esophagus, and cruse.

The surgeon identifies the left vagus nerve coursing anteriorly. Fat obscuring the lesser curve on the anterior stomach is removed to clarify the anatomy. An ethabond suture is placed at the gastroesophageal junction. Mediastinal areolar tissue is divided circumferentially to mobilize the esophagus, ensuring an adequate intraabdominal esophageal length of three to four centimeters.

The surgeon performs a cruroplasty, bringing the left and right crura together posteriorly with a plugeted ethobond suture to recreate the hiatus. The fundus is then brought posteriorly around the esophagus, with the left vagus nerve visible coursing anteriorly. The shoe shine maneuver is executed to symmetrically position the fundus.

Three sutures are placed to secure the fundus to the left side of the esophagus, ensuring the left vagus nerve is protected. The surgeon uses a sliding slip knot technique, starting with a square knot and adjusting the suture orientation by tugging the exiting leg. The knot is locked by pulling the suture ends in opposite directions. Subsequently, the right side of the fundoplication is secured to the esophagus with three interrupted ethabon sutures. Two clips are placed on the proximal sutures to mark the cephalad aspect of the wrap for future identification on x-ray images.
